explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF; leaves row-count chatter enabled, increasing client and network noise.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF; allows some run-time errors to leave a transaction partially applied.
SELECT * from Sales.Orders couples callers to every current and future column of the table.
SELECT TOP (10) returns an arbitrary set of rows because no ORDER BY is specified.
DISTINCT used on OrderID which is already guaranteed unique by the primary key.
WITH (NOLOCK) can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated rows.
YEAR() function applied directly to the OrderDate column prevents a simple range seek.
Implicit conversion occurs when comparing int column CustomerID with nvarchar input @CustomerIdText.
OR-based optional filtering on @CustomerID makes cardinality estimation and seeking difficult.
LIKE pattern starting with % prevents a normal index seek on the Comments column.
NOT IN subquery can produce unexpected results if the subquery (Sales.Invoices) returns NULL values.
Wrapping CustomerID in ISNULL() makes the predicate non-sargable.
Arithmetic operation (OrderID + 0) prevents direct index matching on OrderID.
Inequality operator (<>) often scans broad portions of the table.
CROSS JOIN omits a join predicate and multiplies Orders by Customers.
Comma-separated table list in FROM clause obscures join predicates.
Correlated scalar subquery executes a lookup for every qualifying order row.
Correlated EXISTS check per outer row could be expressed as a set join.
The CASE expression for HasComments is repeated in both the SELECT list and ORDER BY clause.
ORDER BY 2 refers to a select-list column position which is fragile.
OFFSET/FETCH pagination lacks an explicit, stable ORDER BY tie-breaker.
UNION used between branches that are already disjoint by construction (OrderID=1 and OrderID=2).
COUNT(*) > 0 used when only existence is needed.
SELECT @OrderID = o.OrderID silently accepts multiple rows and keeps the last value.
Use of a table variable (@OrderIds) to hold data may result in poor cardinality estimates.
SELECT INTO #ImplicitOrders creates a temp-table schema implicitly instead of declaring it explicitly.
Uses a cursor for work that can be expressed set-wise.
WHILE loop performs iterative work instead of a set-based operation.
Building SQL string by concatenating @CustomerID into executable SQL text.
User-supplied input (@CustomerID) is concatenated directly into a dynamic SQL string before execution.
sp_executesql used with a concatenated string rather than parameterized inputs.
Explicitly created temporary table (#Scratch) is not dropped before the procedure ends.
CATCH block neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status/raises an error.
Use of RAISERROR for new error paths instead of THROW.
Use of PRINT for diagnostics which is buffered and unsuitable for structured diagnostics.
Business identifier (CustomerID = 1) is embedded directly in SQL.
Unlabelled date constant ('20160101') embedded in query logic.
Date string converted without an explicit style parameter or format.
COALESCE used in a join predicate between Sales.Orders and Sales.Invoices.
Self-join on Sales.Orders solely to retrieve the same key (OrderID).
Sorting by a wide text column (Comments) which may be unselective.
Aggregating the entire Sales.OrderLines table without a restrictive filter.
Row-level predicate (ol.OrderID > 0) placed in HAVING instead of WHERE.
ORDER BY on a query guaranteed to return one row (COUNT(*)).
Use of MERGE statement which has documented concurrency and correctness edge cases.
UPDATE statement where SET expression (Comments = Comments) evaluates to the current value.
DELETE with a broad, non-key predicate (Quantity = -1).
WAITFOR DELAY intentionally blocks a worker instead of using asynchronous scheduling.
BEGIN TRANSACTION not wrapped in a BEGIN TRY ... BEGIN CATCH block.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT changes session state and introduces fragile identity-management coupling.
Calling system routine sp_who without a schema qualifier.
Changing session setting (SET ROWCOUNT) and not restoring it.
LIKE pattern starting with % prevents a normal index seek on CustomerName.
Building SQL string by concatenating @CustomerName into executable SQL text.
User-supplied input (@CustomerName) is concatenated directly into a dynamic SQL string before execution.
Unlabelled date constant ('2016-01-01') embedded in query logic.
WHERE column = NULL used instead of IS NULL; will return no rows.
Scalar user-defined function dbo.GetCustomerType called in a SELECT list, forcing row-by-row evaluation.
CATCH block neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status/raises an error.
Explicit table/index hint (WITH (INDEX = 1)) used in production code.
Excessively nested subqueries (3 levels deep) should be refactored to CTEs or temp tables.
SELECT * from dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti couples callers to every current and future column.
